questions.question: Explain the getState() method in Java threads and its return type.
keyPoints: getState() is a method in Thread class
Returns Thread.State enum
Provides current state of thread at query time
States: NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED

questions.question: Differentiate between WAITING and TIMED_WAITING states in Java threads.
keyPoints: WAITING: indefinite waiting period
TIMED_WAITING: specified waiting period
Methods causing WAITING: wait(), join(), LockSupport.park()
Methods causing TIMED_WAITING: sleep(), wait(time), join(time), parkNanos()

questions.question: What is the difference between getState() and isAlive() methods?
keyPoints: getState() returns Thread.State enum
isAlive() returns boolean
isAlive() returns true for RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING
isAlive() returns false for NEW and TERMINATED
getState() gives more detailed state information

questions.question: Explain the NEW and TERMINATED thread states with examples.
keyPoints: NEW: Thread created but start() not called
Example: Thread t = new Thread(r); // t.getState() = NEW
TERMINATED: run() method completed
Example: After t.start(); t.join(); // t.getState() = TERMINATED
Cannot restart a terminated thread

questions.question: When does a thread enter the BLOCKED state? Give an example.
keyPoints: BLOCKED when waiting for monitor lock
Enters synchronized block/method but lock not available
Also when waiting to re-enter after exiting synchronized block
Example: Two threads, one in synchronized block, other trying to enter gets BLOCKED

questions.question: Write a Java program to demonstrate obtaining thread state using getState().
keyPoints: Create a thread
Before start(): shows NEW
After start(): shows RUNNABLE
With sleep(): shows TIMED_WAITING
After completion: shows TERMINATED
Use Thread.State enum comparison

questions.question: Explain all six states of a thread in Java with the methods that cause transition to each state.
keyPoints: NEW - Thread created, start() not called
RUNNABLE - After start(), thread is ready/running
BLOCKED - Waiting for monitor lock (synchronized)
WAITING - Indefinite wait (wait(), join(), LockSupport.park())
TIMED_WAITING - Timed wait (sleep(), wait(time), join(time), parkNanos())
TERMINATED - run() completed normally or exception
Include code examples for each transition

questions.question: Write a comprehensive Java program that demonstrates thread states by creating multiple threads in different scenarios and printing their states.
keyPoints: Main thread to create and start threads
Thread1: simple run (RUNNABLE)
Thread2: use sleep (TIMED_WAITING)
Thread3: wait on object (WAITING)
Thread4: try to enter synchronized block (BLOCKED)
Use getState() to print states at various points
Include proper synchronization and delays
Show expected output

questions.question: Explain the difference between BLOCKED, WAITING, and TIMED_WAITING states with practical scenarios and code examples.
keyPoints: BLOCKED: monitor lock contention, synchronized keyword
WAITING: indefinite, Object.wait(), Thread.join(), LockSupport.park()
TIMED_WAITING: with timeout, Thread.sleep(), wait(timeout), join(timeout)
Practical scenario for BLOCKED: two threads, one in sync block
Practical scenario for WAITING: producer-consumer with wait()
Practical scenario for TIMED_WAITING: waiting with timeout
Code demonstrating each scenario

questions.question: Discuss the importance of obtaining thread state in concurrent applications. How would you use getState() for debugging and monitoring?
keyPoints: Thread state helps diagnose deadlocks and performance issues
Identify threads stuck in BLOCKED (lock contention)
Monitor threads in WAITING state (potential deadlock)
Use getState() in thread dumps for debugging
Can detect infinite loops or starvation
Use in logging for production monitoring
Example code showing state monitoring in a thread pool
